result:
There is a moment, late at night, when the city finally exhales. The freeways thin to a silver thread of taillights, the last barista wipes the steam wand for the final time, and the neon laundromat on the corner begins its electric blinking that will continue until dawn, whether anyone watches or not. I like to slip out in that hush, wearing the rattiest sweater I own—wool so abused it looks like it survived a war fought by moths—and walk the grid of residential streets where the maples have cracked the sidewalks into tectonic plates. The dog at 312 Stafford always hurls himself at the fence as I pass, not in anger but in existential protest, as if to say, “Explain to me again why I am a dog and you are not.” I salute him; he never tires of the question.

A thin, cold mist is rising tonight, the kind that makes streetlights look like drowned stars. I think of my grandmother, who believed fog was simply the earth dreaming aloud. She would sit on the screened porch of her Illinois farmhouse, shelling peas into a dented bowl, humming hymns she no longer believed but still loved for their minor chords. When I visited as a boy, she let me sleep in the attic where the rafters smelled of cedar and mouse nests. I read Choose-Your-Own-Adventure books by flashlight, flipping to page 86 to escape quicksand, to page 44 to pilot a hijacked spaceship, never once choosing the ending where I simply walked home and apologized to Mom. Choice was currency; I spent it recklessly.

Now, decades later, every major decision I have made feels like one of those flashlight moments: a trembling circle of light on a single paragraph, the rest of the pages unread, possibly blank. I chose a college because the recruiter wore a green corduroy jacket the exact shade of a crayon I loved at age five. I accepted a job because the elevator smelled like burnt coffee and ambition, and the woman who would become my supervisor laughed at my joke about semicolons. I married a woman who mispronounced “cinematographer” the first time we met, her tongue tripping over the syllables like a drunk calf, because I suddenly understood that flaws could be doorways instead of walls. Each choice felt like turning to a new page, yet the alternate plots still shadow me like mute ghosts: the me who joined the Peace Corps, the me who never left his grandmother’s attic, the me who said no instead of yes and yes instead of sorry.

Tonight the fog thickens until the houses across the street dissolve into watercolor approximations of themselves. A motion-sensing light snaps on, trapping me in a cone of sterile white. I am briefly a lone actor on an empty stage, spotlighted for an audience of earthworms. The absurdity makes me laugh, and the laugh drifts outward, a small warm coin dropped into the night’s vast coffer. Somewhere a window creaks open; a man coughs the cough of someone who has not yet decided whether to be angry at the disturbance. I keep walking, because walking is what I do when the apartment walls start reciting my failures in surround sound.

At the corner I discover someone has built a tiny free-library box on a reclaimed mahogany leg: “TAKE A BOOK / LEAVE A BOOK / OR JUST COME BACK LATER.” Inside sits a water-warped copy of Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance, a stapled pamphlet on seed saving, and—astonishingly—a spiral notebook labeled “ADD ONE PAGE.” The first sheet is already filled with purple ink: “To whoever finds this: I left my passport on a train in Lyon and never went back. I now sell soap at farmer’s markets and dream in three languages. Tell me the last thing you lost.” I rummage for a pen, find none, and so I tear a blank page planning to reply at home. But by the time I reach my block the paper has become a grocery list—eggs, cilantro, something called “self-forgiveness”—and I realize I have already forgotten what I intended to write. Loss, it seems, is contagious.

Back inside, the radiator clanks like a drunken blacksmith. My cat—official name Esmeralda, nickname You Little Asshole—performs her mandatory figure-eights around my ankles, purring in Morse code that roughly translates to FEED ME/IGNORE ME/ADORE ME. I obey in reverse order. While the tea kettle begins its small seismic rumble, I open my laptop to discover 312 unread messages, a number so symmetrical it feels fake. Subject lines range from the urgent (“Your domain expires in 0 days”) to the delusional (“Hot singles near your area await your profile update”). I close the lid; the screen sighs into darkness. The kettle achieves self-actualization; I pour its scream over a bag of chamomile that has been waiting patiently since Obama’s first term.

Steam fogs my glasses, and without sight I momentarily inhabit a world constructed entirely of smell and sound: wet wool, rusted pipes, the upstairs neighbor’s late-night workout video which consists, mystifyingly, of nothing but jumping jacks. I imagine her apartment as a low-budget space station, zero-gravity achieved through pure cardio willpower. My grandmother would have said she’s trying to jump her way back into a past life. I make a mental note to tape a note to her door: “Whatever you’re leaping from, I hope you land.”

When I remove my glasses, the city reassembles itself in softened edges. I think about posting this moment on social media—caption: “existence in 480p”—but the urge passes. Documentation has become a second skeleton, all clicks and metadata weighing more than the original bone. Instead I carry my cup to the window and peer down at the street I just walked. The laundromat sign pulses pink, pink, pink, a mechanical heartbeat. Somewhere inside, a forgotten sock has completed its solitary orbit and is now beginning the next. I send it silent solidarity; we are both fibers untethered from our pairs, still absorbing the world’s lint, still useful for warmth if not for matching.

The tea cools. Esmeralda settles on the radiator like a loaf of bread that has achieved nirvana. I retrieve the torn grocery-page, smooth its wrinkles, and begin to write: “Dear Lyon Train, I lose the same thing every day: the version of me who boards you. I keep his unused ticket in my bedside drawer, a paper tongue that never speaks. Tonight I walked through fog thick enough to swallow sound and added ‘self-forgiveness’ to a list between eggs and cilantro. If you see him—the man who left you—tell him he can come home. Or not. Either way, I’m learning to walk in holes that fit only one foot.” I sign it with the date, fold it into a paper airplane of questionable aerodynamics, and launch it from the windowsill. It nosedives into the pot of a struggling pothos, which accepts the offering without comment.

Tomorrow I will wake to alarms that sound like rejected techno riffs, will answer at least four of the 312 emails, will misplace my keys and find them exactly where logic said they should be. I will sit in meetings where nouns are turned into verbs and verbs into revenue. I will smile at coworkers while inwardly reciting grocery lists that double as prayers. But tonight, for one more hour, I remain the walking residue of fog and radiator steam, a creature halfway between attic and elevator, between the page I chose and the pages I didn’t. The laundromat sign keeps blinking, and somewhere a dog keeps asking the only question that matters. I do not have an answer, but I have legs that move forward, a heart that refuses the neat epilogue, and a city that exhales permission to keep choosing, badly, beautifully, again.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
